retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Classic tumor suppressor genes (TSGs) are recessive at the cellular level, with inactivation of both alleles typically found in tumors. Inheritance of a solitary mutant allele increases tumor susceptibility because only a single additional inactivating event is necessary for complete loss of gene function. The same gene is often inactivated in sporadic cancers. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor suppressor genes are important genes that act within the genome to regulate several cellular functions. These genes can be broadly classified based on their role in cell growth/cell cycle progression, cell proliferation, DNA repair mechanisms, and other crucial cellular signaling functions such as the apoptosis induction. Without functional tumor suppressor genes, there is a high risk of dysregulated cell growth that is a well-known mechanism for the development of cancers. [1] Loss of function mutations in tumor suppressor genes has been identified in many types of cancers, including ovarian, lung, colorectal, head and neck, pancreatic, uterine, breast, and bladder cancer. [2] [3] [4] [5] [6] There are even familial cancer syndromes associated with the loss of function germline mutations of specific tumor suppressor genes like Li-Fraumeni syndrome with the loss of TP53. [7] Extensive research is underway to understand these genes and their relationship to cancers to facilitate the development of novel targets for specific cancer types.

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: CDKN2A: This complex encodes two tumor suppressor proteins, p16/INK4a, and ARF, which augments RB function and stabilizes p53, respectively. [34] Loss-of-function germline mutations in this gene occur in autosomal dominant familial melanoma. A biallelic loss-of-function presents in multiple cancers including melanomas, leukemias, and carcinomas.

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: WT1: WT1 gene encodes for transcription factors required for normal genitourinary tissue development. Wilms tumor, pediatric kidney cancer, is associated with a germline loss-of-function mutation in this gene. Sporadic Wilms tumor also correlates with similar WT1 mutations. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Many tumor suppressor genes have been the object of studies, and there are likely many more that have yet to be discovered. The mechanisms of each tumor suppressor gene and its protein products are complex and interrelated to other cell signaling pathways, but some better-known mechanisms appear below.

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: BRCA 1, BRCA 2, PARP-1: BRCA1 and BRCA2 are tumor suppressor genes that encode proteins involved in the repair of DNA double-strand breaks through the homologous recombination repair pathway. [31]  PARP-1 encodes a protein that assists with the repair of single-stranded breaks in the DNA. Without functional proteins that repair DNA, the cell cycle continues to pass along defective and mutated genetic material that leads to aberrant daughter cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: TP53 Tumor Suppressor Gene: The TP53 tumor suppressor gene is also known as the “guardian of the genome” as it serves to monitor for cellular stress like anoxia, identify DNA damage, or inappropriate signaling by mutated oncoproteins. [18] [19] The TP53 gene encodes for the p53 protein, which controls the expression of proteins and their activity in cell cycle arrest, cellular senescence, DNA repair, and apoptosis. Loss of p53 can cause continued cell replication despite DNA damage and failure to activate programmed cell death. [20] The DNA damage is perceived by complexes comprising kinases of the ATM/ATR family. [21] These kinases phosphorylate p53, releasing it from inhibitors such as MDM2. The active p53 upregulated the expressions of important proteins like cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitor p21, which causes G1-S checkpoint arrest of the cell cycle. In instances where the DNA damage is not repairable, p53 induces events like activating the BAX gene, which encodes a pro-apoptotic protein that finally lead to cellular apoptosis or senescence. It also works to inhibit the BCL2 anti-apoptotic gene and stimulates the release of cytochrome c from the mitochondria. Cytochrome c activates caspases within the cell responsible for its eventual degradation. Similar to RB, p53 can be inactivated by viral oncoproteins like the E6 protein of HPV, thus revoking the antiproliferative and other important cellular effects. Most of the cancers demonstrate a biallelic loss-of-function mutation in TP53. Uncommon patients with Li-Fraumeni syndrome have a very high incidence of a wide variety of cancers like breast cancer, soft-tissue, and bone sarcomas, and brain tumors since they inherit one defective copy of TP53. [22] [23] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: CDH1 (E-cadherin): Normal cells stop proliferating once they come into contact with neighboring cells, which helps to maintain the structure and architecture of the tissue, referred to as contact inhibition. Mediation of cell-to-cell contact in many tissues is the function of a group of proteins called cadherins. [25] E-cadherin (epithelial cadherin) regulates contact inhibition by binding to a key component of the WNT signaling pathway, ß-catenin. This binding prevents E-cadherin from translocating to the nucleus of the cell, stopping it from activating transcription of pro-growth target genes. [26] Overall, this interaction regulates the morphology and organization of epithelial cell linings. Autosomal dominant familial gastric carcinoma is associated with a germline loss-of-function in this gene. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Points to Remember
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor suppressor genes function to either repress or inhibit the cell cycle or promote apoptosis. The more specific functions of tumor suppressor proteins fall into several categories, including [1] [21] : Inhibition of mitogenic signaling pathways Inhibition of cell cycle progression Inhibition of “pro-growth” programs of metabolism and angiogenesis Inhibition of invasion and metastasis Stabilization of the genome DNA repair factors Induction of apoptosis

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor suppressor genes can be broadly divided into five types functionally [10] : Genes that encode intracellular proteins that are crucial in controlling the progression of cell cycle stages - (e.g., pRB and p16) [11] Genes that encode receptors or signal transducers which orchestrate signals that inhibit cell proliferation [e.g., adenomatous polyposis coli (APC) and transforming growth factor (TGF)-β] [12] Genes that encode the checkpoint-control proteins, which are useful in triggering cell cycle arrest in case of DNA damage or chromosomal defects [e.g., p16, p14, and breast cancer type 1 susceptibility protein (BRCA1)] [13] Genes that encode proteins useful for the induction of apoptosis (e.g., p53) [14] Genes that encode proteins involved in the repair of DNA [e.g., DNA mismatch repair protein 2 (MSH2) and p53] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: NF1 and NF2: NF1 gene encodes for neurofibromin 1, which is a GTPase that functions as a negative regulator of RAS. A germline loss of function mutation of this gene causes Neurofibromatosis type 1, an autosomal dominant disorder associated with the formation of neurofibromas, brain tumors like optic gliomas, and malignant nerve sheath tumors peripherally. [28] [29] NF2 encodes neurofibromin 2 (also known as merlin), which is a cytoskeletal protein associated with contact inhibition. Loss of function mutations of this gene leads to neurofibromatosis type 2, which is also an autosomal dominant disorder associated with an increased risk of bilateral schwannomas among other tumors. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Phosphatase and Tensin Homolog ( PTEN ) Gene: PTEN gene encodes a lipid phosphatase that negatively regulates the phosphoinositide-3-kinase (PI3K)-AKT and the target of mTOR signaling pathways. These pathways are vital for cell proliferation, cell cycle progression, and apoptosis. [24] The PTEN protein also functions to keep migration, adhesion, and angiogenesis in check. It also plays a role in the overall stabilization of the genome. A biallelic loss-of-function is common in diverse cancers. Cowden syndrome is an autosomal dominant disorder resulting from germline loss-of-function mutations of this gene and correlates with a higher risk of breast and endometrial cancer

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Retinoblastoma (RB): RB gene, also called ‘Governor of the Cell Cycle,’ encodes the RB protein that, when hypophosphorylated, binds and inhibits E2F transcriptions factors. [16] These transcription factors regulate genes that are essential for cells to pass from G1 to the S phase of the cell cycle. Typical growth factor signaling causes RB hyperphosphorylation and inactivation, thus causing cell cycle progression. [8] A variety of mechanisms like loss-of-function mutations affecting RB, CDK4, and cyclin D gene amplification, cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitors loss (p16/INK4a), and inhibition of RB by the binding of viral oncoproteins (E7 protein of HPV) can revoke the antiproliferative effect of RB in cancers. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: VHL: VHL gene encodes a component of a ubiquitin ligase, which is involved in the degradation of hypoxia-induced factors (HIFs). These are transcription factors that alter the expression of genes in response to hypoxia. Von Hippel-Lindau syndrome, an autosomal dominant disorder, is associated with loss-of-function germline mutations of this gene and poses a high risk of developing renal cell carcinoma and pheochromocytoma. [38] [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: APC: The APC gene encodes a tumor suppressor protein that negatively regulates the WNT signaling pathway. This regulation leads to enhancement of the formation of a complex that degrades β-catenin that is involved in the regulation and co-ordination of cell-cell adhesion and gene transcription. [32] [33] The APC mutation is present in familial adenomatous polyposis, an autosomal dominant disorder where thousands of colonic polyps develop with early onset of colon carcinoma. The tumor development is associated with a loss of a single normal APC allele.

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 257
retrieved_docs.content: importance of assessing metastatic potential even when the primary lesion’s benign versus malignant nature is unclear. Collectively, these interrelated entities form a hierarchical and functional network that connects genetic syndromes, primary gastrointestinal cancers, various metastatic malignancies, and skin melanomas, providing essential context for accurate diagnosis, staging, and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1899
retrieved_docs.content: Bard‑Pic syndrome is linked to pancreatic cancer, indicating that this genetic or clinical syndrome has a mapped association with the development of cancer of the pancreas. Recognizing this connection is important for clinicians, as patients diagnosed with Bard‑Pic syndrome may require heightened surveillance for pancreatic malignancy and tailored management strategies to address the potential increased risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1908
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are the abnormal cells that define the disease known as سرطانة العظم (bone cancer).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 240
retrieved_docs.content: similarly defined as a primary malignant neoplastic proliferation. In addition, an inverse‑is‑a relationship links the entity “سرطانة توتية” to the primary malignant tumor morphology (“ورم خبيث أولي”), further emphasizing its classification as a type of primary malignant neoplasm. Together, these relationships illustrate how various primary malignant neoplasms across different organs are unified by common pathological processes, shared morphological descriptors, and hierarchical links to broader cancer concepts, aiding in consistent classification and clinical understanding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1107
retrieved_docs.content: Bard‑Pic syndrome is associated with pancreatic cancer, indicating that this genetic or clinical syndrome maps to a malignant neoplasm of the pancreas. Carcinoma of the head of the pancreas is described as an “الورم السرطاني” (cancerous tumor) in its associated morphology and is also mapped to pancreatic cancer, identifying it as a specific histologic subtype located in the pancreatic head. Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas similarly maps to pancreatic cancer, representing another terminology for a malignant tumor arising in the same region. Together, Bard‑Pic syndrome, carcinoma of the head of the pancreas, and primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas all refer to malignant pancreatic disease, with the latter two emphasizing the anatomical location (head of the pancreas) and morphological characteristics of the tumor, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, classification, and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion is recognized as a diagnostic finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating that the disease has spread beyond its primary site. In the context of سرطانة العظم, Tumor cells, malignant are identified as the abnormal cellular component that drives the malignancy. Together, the presence of malignant tumor cells and the detection of metastatic lesions provide critical information for diagnosing, staging, and managing سرطانة العظم, guiding therapeutic decisions and prognostic assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2918
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells represent the abnormal cellular component that defines and drives the disease known as السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer), indicating that the presence of these atypical, cancerous cells is the hallmark of this specific malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2884
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant, are identified as abnormal cells of disease and are classified under the category “cancer; other and unspecified primary,” indicating that these cells represent a form of cancer for which the primary site cannot be determined. This classification underscores the diagnostic uncertainty surrounding such tumor cells and highlights the need for further pathological evaluation to clarify their nature and guide appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2902
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are abnormal cells that characterize cancers classified as other and unspecified primary, indicating that these atypical cells constitute the pathological basis of such unspecified primary malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2554
retrieved_docs.content: Bard-Pic syndrome and primary malignant neoplasm of head of pancreas are both mapped to cancer of pancreas, indicating that Bard-Pic syndrome is related to pancreatic cancer and that the primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas represents a specific form of pancreatic cancer. This connection highlights Bard-Pic syndrome as a risk factor for pancreatic malignancy and underscores the clinical relevance of identifying tumors located in the pancreatic head.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2372
retrieved_docs.content: cancer; other and unspecified primary and سرطان الغدة الدرقية are both classified as subtypes of ورم صماوي خبيث, which denotes a malignant tumor. This relationship indicates that these specific cancers fall under the broader category of malignant tumors, sharing the characteristic of uncontrolled, invasive cell growth. Recognizing them as forms of ورم صماوي خبيث helps guide diagnostic evaluation, treatment planning, and prognostic assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2901
retrieved_docs.content: Benign tumor cells are not considered a finding associated with ovarian cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2004
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Abnormal” is used as an interpretation of فقر دم فانكوني (Fanconi anemia), indicating that the clinical and laboratory findings associated with this genetic disorder are considered abnormal. Fanconi anemia is a hereditary condition characterized by bone‑marrow failure, congenital anomalies, and a heightened risk of malignancies, and the descriptor “Abnormal” reflects the pathological nature of its presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2888
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a diagnostic finding of cancer of the ovary, indicating the presence of abnormal tissue within the ovarian region. Similarly, Tumor cells, malignant are identified as the abnormal cellular component of cancer of the ovary, representing the malignant cells that drive the disease process. Together, these pathological entities underscore the cellular abnormalities that characterize ovarian cancer and are essential for its diagnosis and understanding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2581
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, malignant and tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant are both classified as abnormal cells of disease and are linked to cancer; other and unspecified primary, with a specific association to pancreatic cancer (السرطان البنكرياسي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2734
retrieved_docs.content: Female Reproductive System Disorder is a broad clinical category that includes a range of conditions affecting the female reproductive organs. Within this category, cancer of the uterus represents a malignant neoplasm of the uterine tissue, while ورم مهبلي حميد (benign vaginal tumor) denotes a non‑malignant growth in the vagina. Recognizing both uterine cancer and benign vaginal tumors as specific manifestations of Female Reproductive System Disorder highlights their shared relevance to gynecologic health and underscores the importance of tailored diagnostic and therapeutic strategies for each condition.
